summary:
Gobekli Tepe, located on a limestone ridge in southeastern Anatolia near the modern city of Sanliurfa in Turkey, is the oldest known example of monumental religious architecture in the world. Excavations begun in 1994 by Klaus Schmidt revealed massive T-shaped limestone pillars, some standing over five meters tall and weighing up to ten tons, arranged in circular enclosures and decorated with intricate relief carvings of animals including foxes, aurochs, snakes, cranes, and abstract symbols. The site dates to approximately 9500–8000 BCE.

What makes Gobekli Tepe extraordinary is its chronological position: it was constructed before the widespread adoption of agriculture in the region. The builders appear to have been hunter-gatherers or communities in the earliest stages of the [[event:1|Neolithic transition]], organized enough to quarry, transport, and erect enormous stone structures but not yet fully settled farmers. This challenges earlier assumptions that monumental architecture required an agricultural surplus as a prerequisite.

The site encompasses multiple enclosures built in successive phases. The earliest and most elaborate structures belong to Layer III, while later Layer II enclosures are smaller and less sophisticated — a reversal of the expected trajectory of architectural development. Around 8000 BCE, the site was deliberately and carefully buried, its pillars and enclosures backfilled with rubble, though the reasons for this intentional interment remain debated among researchers.

Archaeobotanical evidence from the surrounding region suggests that the intensive gathering and perhaps early cultivation of wild einkorn wheat occurred in the vicinity, leading some scholars to propose that the communal labor demands of building and maintaining Gobekli Tepe may have been a driver — rather than a product — of agricultural intensification. The site also shows evidence of large-scale feasting, with animal bones from wild species deposited in enormous quantities.

Gobekli Tepe has fundamentally altered scholarly understanding of the cognitive and social capacities of pre-agricultural humans. The symbolic complexity of its carvings, the coordinated effort required for its construction, and its apparent function as a regional cult center all point to rich ritual and social life long before cities or writing.
